topic: reflecting on the year — kitchen is hard but she loves it

turns.content: it's almost 3am and I'm sitting on my couch with a glass of wine and I just feel like... talking, I guess. not about anything specific. just about everything
I'm here. 3am wine and everything on your mind — go wherever you want with it.

I've been doing this — the kitchen, the life, all of it — for over a decade now. and some nights after service I come home and I sit down and my feet hurt and my back hurts and my hands are covered in little burns and cuts and I think... is this going to be the rest of my life? standing in a hot room for fourteen hours making food that disappears in fifteen minutes?
That's a heavy question for 3am. The physical toll alone is real — kitchen work destroys your body over time. What usually pulls you out of that spiral?

honestly? the next service. that's the sick part. I'll have this existential crisis at 3am, sleep four hours, wake up, and the second I walk into the kitchen and start prepping I feel alive again. the smell of shallots in butter. the sound of a knife on a board. the rhythm of the line when everything is clicking. putain, it's like a drug. you can't explain it to people who don't do this

It sounds like the thing that exhausts you is the same thing that sustains you. That's not sick — a lot of people in demanding creative work describe the same cycle. The low hits hard because the high is so good. Do you think you'd ever walk away from it?

no. that's the answer and I've known it for a long time. I've thought about it — what else would I do? office work? I'd lose my mind in a week. I'm not built for a desk. I'm built for a kitchen. my hands know what to do before my brain catches up. I can butcher a duck in four minutes. I can taste a broth and tell you exactly what's missing. this is the only thing I've ever been really, truly good at

There's something grounding about knowing that with that much certainty. A lot of people spend their whole lives looking for a thing that fits them that completely. The question isn't whether you'll stay in the kitchen — it's what the next version of your kitchen life looks like.

da, that's the real question. and I don't have the answer yet. there are days when I feel like I'm on the edge of something big, like everything I've been learning and building is about to come together. and then there are nights like tonight where I'm just tired. bone tired. the kind of tired that sleep doesn't fix
Both can be true at the same time. You can be exhausted and still be moving toward something. The tiredness doesn't negate the momentum — it's the cost of it. What's weighing on you most right now?

I think... the feeling that time is moving fast. I'm thirty-three. in kitchen years that's not old but it's not young either. the body doesn't recover like it used to. I used to do doubles and feel fine. now a double leaves me wrecked for two days. and I look at the twenty-two-year-old commis running around the kitchen with all that energy and I think, merde, that was me ten years ago. where did it go?

But you have something that twenty-two-year-old doesn't — a decade of knowledge, instinct, and refinement. Energy fades but skill compounds. At twenty-two you had the legs; at thirty-three you have the palate, the eye, the judgment. That's the trade, and it's a good one.

you're right. I know you're right. and when I'm actually cooking — when I'm in the zone, tasting, plating, adjusting — I don't feel thirty-three. I don't feel anything except the food. that's the magic of it. the kitchen is the only place where my brain shuts up. no anxiety, no doubt, just the work. chop, sear, sauce, plate. repeat. it's meditation with knives

"Meditation with knives" — that's a perfect way to put it. That flow state is what keeps people in the hardest professions. It sounds like the kitchen isn't just your career, it's your way of being present. The 3am doubts are real, but they're the noise. The signal is what happens when you pick up a knife.

okay now I'm getting sentimental and I've had too much wine for sentimentality. but thank you. seriously. sometimes I just need to say this stuff out loud — or type it, whatever — to remember that I chose this life and I'd choose it again. every burn, every broken sauce, every screaming chef, every 2am walk home in the snow. all of it. c'est ma vie and I wouldn't trade it. bonne nuit for real this time

You'd choose it again. That's the only answer that matters. The burns heal, the sauces get remade, the chefs stop screaming eventually. But the love for the craft — that's the thing that stays. Bonne nuit, Nadia. Go sleep. Tomorrow the kitchen is waiting, and it's yours.
